title: Today's AI Brief: Why the Vatican Invited Anthropic to the Pope's AI Encyclical Presentation
summary: The Vatican's unexpected invite to Anthropic for the Pope's AI encyclical presentation, and what it means for AI adoption and safety.
imageAlt: Image from source article for Why the Vatican Invited Anthropic to the Pope’s AI Encyclical Presentation
imageCaption: Why the Vatican Invited Anthropic to the Pope’s AI Encyclical Presentation
imageSourceTitle: Why the Vatican Invited Anthropic to the Pope’s AI Encyclical Presentation

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to today's AI Brief. I've got some interesting news to share with you, particularly around the intersection of AI and, surprisingly, the Vatican.

First up, the Vatican made a rather unexpected move by inviting Christopher Olah, co-founder of Anthropic, to speak at the presentation of Pope Leo XIV's first encyclical on artificial intelligence. For those who might not know, an encyclical is essentially a circular letter sent to all Catholic bishops by the Pope. This one's focus on AI is quite a statement, given the Church's historical views on science and technology. The fact that Anthropic, a company known for its work in AI safety, was invited to participate signals a significant interest in how AI can be developed and used responsibly.

Another piece of news caught my eye - the growing adoption of enterprise-level AI agents. According to a piece in MIT Technology Review, there's a bit of a disconnect between the ambition of companies to leverage AI and their actual execution. About 85% of organizations express a desire to be more 'agentic' with AI, but getting there seems to be another story. This gap suggests that while the potential of AI is widely recognized, the path to realizing that potential is fraught with challenges, from technical hurdles to perhaps more importantly, organizational design issues.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
